text: ABBEY-NEW. See New-Abbey.
ABBEY-POINT, a promontory 98 ft. high, at the entrance of the harbour of Napakeang, on the coast of Loo Choo. A reef extends off this point.—Beechey.
ABBEY-SAINT-BATHAN'S, a moorland p. in Berwickshire, so called from an abbey founded here in 1170. Pop. 146. Area 5,000 acres.
ABBEYSHRUEL, a barony and a p. in co. Longford. Pop. of p. 1,283. Area 2,340 acres.
ABBEYSIDE, a v. and suburb of the town of Dungarvan, co. Waterford. Pop. in 1831, 1,859. See DUNGARVAN.
ABBEYSTROWRY, a p. in co. Cork. Pop. in 1841, of the rural district, excluding that part of the town of Skibbereen which is in this p., 4,358. Area 9,396 acres. See SKIBBEREEN.

ABBIATEGRASSO, or Abbiagrasso, a district and a manufacturing town in the prov. of Pavia, in Venetian Lombardy, in the valley of the Tessino, on the Bereguardo or Naviglio Grande canal, 18 m. NW of Pavia. Pop. 4,500.—The district comprises 25 communes, with a pop. of about 30,000.

ABBITIBBE, a lake of N. America lying under the parallel of 49°, and between 78° and 80° W long., on the high ground which divides the rivers flowing SE into the St. Lawrence, from those flowing N into Hudson's bay. It is about 60 m. in length, and 15 in breadth. The country around, which has only been very partially explored, is occupied by the Abbitibbe Indians. The Hudson's bay company have a station at the E end of the lake, which discharges its waters ABD by the Abbitibbe river, issuing from its W extremity, and flowing into the estuary of the Moose river, or the SW corner of Hudson's bay, after a course of about 250 m.
ABBO, a town of Abyssinia, in the district of Gata, 15 m. S of the junction of the head-streams of the Abai.
ABBOSH, a small Druse town, in the pashalik of Acre, on the Birket-el-Tel river.
ABBOT, a town of Maine, U.S., on the Piscatiquis, 70 m. N by E of Augusta. Pop. 660.

ABBOT'S, a frequent prefix in English topography. Thus we have Abbot's Ann, Abbot's Astley, Abbot's Bickington, Abbot's Bromley, &c. It usually indicates that the manor had formerly been ecclesiastical property.
ABBOTSBURY, a p. and town of Dorsetshire. Pop. 1,005. Area 4,050 acres. The town, a small fishing-port, is 8 m. SW of Dorchester.
ABBOTSHALL, a p. and manufacturing town in Fifeshire. Pop. of p. 4,811, whereof 4,100 belong to the town, which forms a suburb of Kirkcaldy, and is on the line of the Edinburgh and Northern railway.
ABBOTSHAM, a p. in Devonshire, 2 m. W of Bideford. Pop. 414, Area 2,090 acres.
ABBOTSIDE (High and Low), two townships in Aysgarth p., Yorkshire, 6 m. E of Askrigg. Pop. of H. A. 574; of L. A. 166.
ABBOT'S KERSWELL, a p. in Devonshire, 13 m. S of Newton-Abbott, Pop. 433. Area 1,670 acres.

ABBOTS-LANGLEY, a p. and v. in Hertfordshire. Pop. 2,115. Area 5,100 acres. The v. is 2 m. E by S of King's Langley. It was the birth-place of Nicholas de Breakspear who was raised to the papal throne under the name of Adrian IV.
ABBOT'S-LEIGH, a p. in Somersetshire, 3 m. W. of Bristol. Pop. 366. Area 2,150 acres.
ABBOTSLEY, a p. in Huntingdonshire, 4 m. SE of St. Neot's. Pop. 443. Area 2,110 acres.
ABBOTSTON. See ITCHEN-STOKE.

ABBOTSVILLE, the capital since 1839 of the English settlement in Vera Paz in Central America. It is situated on the l. bank of the Rio Polochic at the junction of the Boca Nueva, about 30 m. above the outfall of the Polochic into lake Ysabal. ABBOTTS-ASTON, ABBOTTS-BRAMPTON, ABBOTTS-COTTON, &c. See ASTON-ABBOTTS, BRAMPTONABBOTTS, &c.
ABBOUL, a small river of Livonia, flowing into the Boulder-Aa on the l., 3 m. NE of Volmar.
ABBRADIE, an interior tributary state in the kingdom of Ashanti.
ABBRO, a small island of Livonia, to the S of the island of Oesel, at the entrance of the gulf of Riga.
ABCHERON. See APCHERON.
AB-CHOTOH, a point "where there is a tablespace sufficient for a large encampment" in the pass at the northern extremity of the plain of Khod, in Kalat.—Masson.
ABCOUDE, a v. of Holland, prov. of Utrecht, on the Krom branch of the Amstel. Pop. 1,100.
ABDADTSK, a town of A. Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the l, bank of the Ishim. Pop. 2,000.

ABDAH, a fertile prov. of Marocco, on the W coast, noted for its breed of horses. It has Dukaila on the N and E, the Tensift river on the S, and the Mediterranean on the W. Its chief town is Asafí, or Saffee, in N lat. 32° 18′, W long. 9° 12′. Area 2,000 sq. m. Pop. estimated at 200,000.
ABDA'LI, the name of an Arab tribe, and of the district on the S coast of Arabia in which Aden is situated. The tribe is estimated at 10,000 persons; and the district at 600 sq. m.
ABDALPOUR. See ABDULPU'R.
ABDAMA, a town in the pashalik of Aleppo. The neighbourhood is fertile, and a large quantity of scammony is prepared here.

ABDEH (EL), or ANJEH, extensive ruins in the Great Western desert of Arabia Petræa, in N lat. 30° 55', supposed by Professor Robinson to be on the line of the Roman road from Ailah to Hebron, and to mark the site of the ancient Eboda.
ABDIE, a p. in Fifeshire. Pop. 1,508; of the principal v., the Grange of Lindores, 166.
ABDIKINI, a town of Bulgaria, on the coast of the Black sea, a little to the S of the embouchure of the Kamtchik; N lat. 42° 55', E long. 27° 54'.
ABDON, a p. in Salop. Pop. 155. Area 710 acres.
ABDON, one of the Papuan islands, forming the group of Aiou, to the N of Waigiu, in the Asiatic archipelago. It is 10 m. in circumf., with a height of 200 ft. above sea-level; and abounds in turtle.
ABDUI, a v. in the Persian prov. of Fars, in the valley of Deshtber, near the foot of the abrupt Pir-i- Zen chain.—Ainsworth, and De Bode.
ABDUL, a town of Cabul, in N lat. 31° 43', E long. 66° 6'.
ABDULABAD, a town of Hindostan, prov. of Oude, 5 m. N of Manickpore.
ABDULAZEEM (SHAH), a v. of Persia, rising amongst the ruins of the city of Rhé, the Rhages of Arrian, and the birth-place of the caliph Hároun-er- Rashed, in the neighbourhood of Tehraun.—Fraser.
ABD-UL-KADIR, an island in the Nile, to the SE of Girgeh.
ABD-UL-KERIM, an Arab tribe in Marocco, who inhabit the district to the W of the sources of the Muluyah.—Also a v. in Little Tibet on the road from Fezabad to Yarcand.

ABD-UL-KU'RE, or Palinurus Shoal, a dangerous rocky shoal, extending 1,850 yards in a NNE and SSW direction, with only 17 ft. water, lying S by E 8 m. of Misenát, on the S coast of Arabia in N lat. 14° 54', E long. 50° 45'.—Haines.

ABD-UL-KURIA, a high rugged granitic island in the Arabian sea, W of Socotra, 17 m. long from E to W, but very narrow, in lat. between 12° 9' and 12° 12' N, and 52° 6' and 52° 28' E long. A rock lies about 12 m. N of this point.—Haines. ABDU-LLAH-GA'U, "a thriving v. of a thieving tribe" in the plain of Meidan in Northern Khorasan, within 2 m. of the town of Meidan.—Fraser in Geog. Journ. viii. 313.
ABDULPU'R, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Haidenabad, on the N bank of the Bimah, 41 m. NE of Bijapúr.
ABDULRAHIM-KHAN, a v. in Beloochistan in the valley of Shawl, at which the ascent to the pass of Kushlak commences. It is 5,500 ft. above sea-level.
ABDU'M, a v. opposite Merawe on the Nile, 14 m. SW of Ed-dukayet.
ABEA, a river of Abyssinia which flows into the Abai, from the S, a little above Broken-bridge.—Also a town of Nubia in Sennaar, 35 m. E of Sennaar.
ABEADH. See ABIAD.
ABEARIS, or ABU-ARISCH, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, the residence of a sheriff. It is 80 m. N of Saade. Pop. 6,000.
ABEGERM, a town on the S coast of the Caspian, in the prov. of Mazanderán, SE of Resht.
ABEJAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, 16 m. WNW of Soria.

ABEL (NEBI), a high and steep mountain 12 m. NW of Damascus, surmounted by a ruined building which, according to Arab tradition, marks the spot where the fratricide Cain buried the body of his brother [Pococke]. The origin of this tradition Rosenmuller traces to the compounding of the name of a town which anciently existed in the neighbourhood, Abel or Abila, with the Hebrew Hebel. Ptolemy fixes the Abila of Lysanias 18 m. NW of Damascus; and Pococke, in 1737, found a Greek in- is 5 m. SE of Neath. It is contributory to the Swan- ABE scription in the ruined building on the top of Nebi Abel which mentions "Lysanias, tetrarch of Abilene." But Robinson identifies Abilene with Abela, Abil, or Abila, a ruined town considerably to the N, in the Hauran, in N. lat. 34° 47' and E long. 36°.—Travels in Palestine and Syria ii. 122.
ABELIAGHSKAIA, a bay on the coast of Siberia, between Cape Darnigen and Sviatoi Nose; in about N lat. 72° 30′, E long. 141°.
ABELIN, a fort and v. in the pashalik of Acre, 21 m. W of Tabarieh, and 9 m. ESE of Acre.
ABELOE, a small island on the N coast of Funen.
ABELOVA, a Lutheran v. in Hungary, in the county of Nograd, 14 m. NW of Losontz.

ABENAKIS, a tribe of North American Indians located between the St. Francis and the Chaudiere rivers, and also along the St. John's river and the Restigouche, among the high grounds which divide the streams which fall into the St. Lawrence from those which flow into the Atlantic.
ABENBERG, sometimes known as Klein-Am-
BERG, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 13 m. SSW of Nürnburg. Pop. 1,100.
ABENHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, 4 m. NW of Worms. Pop. 1,224.
ABENOJAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. of New Castile, 20 m. SW of Ciudad-Real, in the Campo de Calatrava, which is watered by the Guadiana and a small affluent bearing the name of this town.
ABENRADE. See APENRADE.
ABENS, a small river in Bavaria, which rises near Aw, in the circle of Izar, and flowing N 25 m. falls into the Danube, on its r. bank, 1 m. N of Neustadt.

ABENSBERG, a district and town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, anciently belonging to the counts of Abensberg celebrated in German history. The district extends over 120 sq. m., and has a population of 15,000. The town is seated on the r. bank of the Abens, and on the S side of the Danube, 16 m. SW of Ratisbon. Pop. 1,300. Here Napoleon, on 26th April, 1809, defeated the Austrian archduke, Louis, and General Hiller, who retired upon Landshut. A. was the birth-place of John Thurmaier, the annalist of Bavaria, who assumed from this place the surname Aventinus.

ABENSEE, or St. Wolfgang, a beautiful little lake in Upper Austria, in the circle of Salzburg. It is 5 m. long, by nearly 1 m. in breadth, and abounds in fish. The abbey and v. of St. Wolfgang-whence it takes its name—is situated on its N shore, 18 m. E of Salzburg. At its W extremity, opposite to St. Wolfgang, is the v. of St. Gilgen.

ABER, a prefix of frequent occurrence in British topography. It seems of Celtic origin, and will be found generally to denote a situation near the mouth of a stream or river. D'Herbelot says that the same prefix, in Persian topography, usually signifies au-dessus, above, an elevated position.

ABER, a p. and v. in Carnarvonshire. Pop. of p. 556. The v. is situated at the mouth of the river Gwyngregin, 5 m. ENE of Bangor, and there is here a ferry across the Lavan sands to Anglesea. Penmaen-mawr, 1,545 ft. in altitude [Caswell], is about 3 m. E from Aber; and in the neighbourhood is a celebrated cataract called Rhayader-mawr.
ABERAERON, or ABERAYRON, a small port at the mouth of the Aeron, in Cardigan bay, 15 m. SW of Aberystwith. Pop. 534.—The Aeron has a course of about 12 m. in length.

ABERAVON, a p. and a borough in Glamorganshire, on the E side of Swansea bay, at the mouth of the Avon. Pop. of the p. in 1831, 573; in 1841, 1,290. This rapid increase is attributed to the formation of an excellent harbour at the outfall of the Avon, and to the extension of the coal, copper, iron, and tin works in the neighbourhood. The borough sea district of boroughs in the election of an M.P.; but its boundaries as a parliamentary borough have been enlarged so as to embrace a pop. in 1841 of 3,665. The annual value of assessed property in this parish in 1842-3 was £2,435.
ABERBROTHWICK. See ARBROATH.
ABERCHIRDER, a v. in the p. of Marnoch, Banffshire, 5 m. NE of Turriff. Pop. 819.

ABER-COBA'D, a ruined town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, about 6 m. E of Behbehán. The v. of Mansurieh appears to have risen upon its ruins, although D'Herbelot considers the prefix Aber to denote its position on elevated ground, and Mansurieh occupies a low site on the banks of the Tab.—De Bode.
ABERCONWAY. See CONWAY.
ABERCORN, a p. in Linlithgowshire. Pop. 5,146; of whom 250 were in the v. of Newton, and 140 in Philpstown.
ABERCROMBIE, or St. Monance, a p. and fishing-village in Fifeshire. Pop. of p. 1,157; of whom 1,029 were in the v., which is 11 m. W of Pittenweem.
ABERCROMBIE, a township of Lower Canada, co. of Terrebonne, 30 m. NW of Montreal. Pop. 200.
ABERCROMBIE, a river of New South Wales, descending from the western side of the Blue Mountains, under the parallel of 34° 49' S, and running W, through Georgiana, apparently to join the Lachlan.

ABERCROMBIE'S ISLAND, a little uninhabited island lying in the mouth of the gulf of Macri, on the coast of Lycia. "It consists," says Dr. E. D. Clarke, who first bestowed a name upon it, "of a single mountain covered with an exuberant vegetation."- Travels, vol. iii. pp. 325-328.
ABERDALGIE, a p. of Perthshire extending along the Earn. Pop. 360. The battle of Duplin was fought here on 12th August, 1332.

ABERDARE, a p. and burgh in Glamorganshire. Pop. 6,471; of whom 1,322 were in the burgh of A., which is 4 m. SW of Merthyr-Tydvil, and is a contributory parliamentary burgh to the Cardiff district. There are extensive iron works in this p. which have raised the pop. from 3,961 in 1831 to its present amount. The Cynon, a tributary of the Taff, waters the vale of Aberdare. From Ynys Cynon 3 m. from A. a canal 6 m, in length runs to join the Cardiff canal.—A branch of the Taff vale railway, about 84 m. in length, opened in August, 1846, at an expense of £34,452, connects A. with the town of Cardiff.
ABERDARON, a p. in Carnarvonshire, at the outfall of the Daron into Cardigan bay. Pop. 1,350.
ABERDDAW, two v. on the Glamorganshire coast of the British channel, within half-a-mile of each other, and sometimes known as East and West Aberthaw.

ABERDEEN, the capital of Aberdeenshire, and the third town of Scotland in importance. It consists strictly speaking of two distinct towns,-Old Aberdeen and New Aberdeen, -which are situated at the distance of about a mile from each other, and in different parishes, and as municipalities have distinct charters and privileges; but are nearly connected by a suburb called Spittal, are included within the same parliamentary boundary, and unite in returning one member to parliament. The pop. of the united burghs in 1707 was 6,500; in 1801, 27,608; in 1831, 58,019; in 1841, 63,262. The number of parliamentary electors in 1839-40 was 2,189; in 1842-43, 2,582.

OLD ABERDEEN, a burgh-of-barony, the seat of a university, and anciently a bishop's see, is situated on the right or south bank of the river Don, to the N of New Aberdeen, in the parish of Old Machar. The name has assumed various orthographies. To the Norsemen, this town was known by the name of Apardion; in the earlier historical monuments of ABE deyn, Aberden, and Habyrdine. It is a place of great antiquity, and was of considerable importance towards the end of the 9th cent. In 1154, David I. translated the episcopal see from Mortlach in Banffshire to this place. In 1336, Edward having invaded Scotland, and led his army as far north as Inverness, on his return sacked and destroyed Aberdeen; but it was gradually rebuilt and considerably enlarged, particularly towards the rising grounds upon which the principal part of the united parliamentary burgh now stands: hence arose the new town or New Aberdeen. The old town, we are now describing, is a small place consisting of little more than a single street, and dependent entirely upon its university. Its population in 1831 was only 1,483; and in 1841, 1,490; but that of the parish, which extends 2 m. up the north side of the Dee, and has an area of 16.5 sq. m. was 9,911 in 1801; 25,107 in 1831; and in 1841, 28,020. The limits of the burgh are of course of small extent, and they appear likewise to have been ill-defined. The revenue in 1832 was only £43 5s. Unlike, however, its younger and overshadowing sister, if it has little property, it has no debts.—The chief edifices are the cathedral and the college. The cathedral of St. Machar was originally founded in 1154; but the building of which the present remains formed a part was begun in 1357. It is said to have been 70 years in progress, but it does not appear to have ever been completed. The choir and transept have disappeared, and the great central tower has long been demolished: the nave only remains, and forms the present parish-church. The west window is a very fine one; and the roof is of oak finely carved. King's college, which is situated at a little distance on the E side of the town, is a handsome fabric of different periods of erection. It was finished in 1500. In 1836 its income amounted to £2,363, whereof £1,215 arose from endowments, and £1,148 from crown-grants. Its bursaries amounted to £1,643. In 1840 it was enriched by a munificent bequest of £11,000. Its library contains about 30,000 vols. NEW ABERDEEN is situated on a rising ground, on the N bank of the Dee, near the mouth of that river, and about 13 m. to the S of the river Don; in N lat. 57° 9', and W long. 2° 6'; 108 m. NNE of Edinburgh; 115 SE of Inverness; and 395 N by W of London. Its pop. in 1801 was 17,597; in 1821, 26,484; in 1831, 32,912; in 1841, 35,260. The annual value of property assessed to income tax in 1842-3, as in the parish of St. Nicholas, was £105,827, whereof £81,162 was on houses, and £3,380 on fisheries.—N. A. is a large and handsome city with spacious streets. Unionstreet is upwards of a m. in length, and of great beauty. Broad-street, in which Marischal college is situated, is celebrated as having been the residence of Lord Byron while under his mother's care. The finest of the modern public buildings is the county-rooms, erected in 1820 at an expense of £11,500. The new markets, opened in 1842, form an immense hall 315 ft. in length, 106 in breadth, and 45 in height. The cross, originally erected in 1686 on the site of a more ancient structure, and renovated in 1841, is considered one of the most complete structures of the kind now in existence. In 1817 the corporation of N. A. became bankrupt; but the town's affairs are now rapidly retrieving. The average revenue of the city was £15,200 in 1832; in 1848-9 it was £17,208. The property of the city was valued at £223,979 in 1832.— The principal manufactures of the town and the adjacent country, prior to 1745, were worsted stockings and coarse woollens; there are now several large establishments for the spinning and weaving of cotton, flax, and wool; also extensive works for the manufacture of machinery. The salmon-fisheries on the Scotland it appears written Aberdon, Aberdoen, Abyr- | Dee and the Don rivers are worth £10,000 per annum, PORTS AND HARBOURS ON THE NORTH EAST COAST OF SCOTLAND NORTH EAST COAST Z Coresca L.B. Burghead Lossiemouth Shire Naira Sh. Moras ff II.

No 1 Grangird's He Fraserburgh Banff S hire Hattray Hd Peterhead Pool Mik Boddam Buchan Ness Girdle N Girdlestone Girdle Ness L. H. (2 Fixed in one Tower) B ay G.H. Swanaton, Edin' * 2 . . and employ many hands; and a large export trade is conducted in corn, eggs, butter, pork, live cattle, and granite stone. The harbour has 16 ft. at neaptides, and 19 ft. of water at spring-tides, and 6,870 ft. of wharfage. Its income in 1849 was £23,387. In 1849 there belonged to the port of N. A. 256 vessels of the total tonnage of 54,400 tons, and 16 steamers of 7,521 tons. Powerful steamers sail regularly between it and London. A wet-dock of 331 acres was opened in 1849, having two entrances, one of 60 ft., the other 70 ft. wide, fit to take in the largest steamer. The arrivals in 1849 were 2,490 vessels of 311,282 tons. The custom dues in 1656 amounted to £80; in 1849, to £94,832. The amount of postage in 1849 was £9,500.—A canal 184 m. in length runs from the harbour to the Don at Inverury. It has an ascent of 168 ft. by 17 locks, of which 15 are near the city; width, 23 ft.; depth 3 ft. 9 in. By the Aberdeen railway, now executing, N. A. will be placed in direct connexion with Dundee, Perth, and Stirling; its railway distance from Edinburgh, via Forfar, Perth, and Stirling, will be 157 m.; from Glasgow 152 m. The length of the line from A. to the Arbroath and Forfar railway at Friockheim, is 491 m. It sends off a branch to Montrose of 3 m.; and another to Brechin of 3\frac{1}{2} m.—The bridges across the Dee and the Don are under the management of the magistrates of N. A. About 1,200 yards above the mouth of the Don is the well-known 'Brig o' Balgownie,' consisting of a single pointed arch of 72 ft. span. A new bridge 500 ft. in length, and consisting of 5 arches, was opened in 1830 at a point 450 yards lower down the river. The stone bridge across the Dee was recently widened from 15 ft. to 26 ft; and a suspension bridge has been thrown across the Dee 2,600 yards lower down the river. N. A. is well situated for effectual drainage, and might be better drained. A sewer 2,250 ft. long and 6 ft. high has just been completed. The lanes are narrow; and there are courts or closes to the number of 168, of which the average breadth is not above 7 ft. The quantity of water supplied to the inhabitants is, on an average of one week, about 570,000 gallons.- Marischal college, founded in 1593, has been recently rebuilt on an extensive plan, at an estimated cost of £25,000. Its library in 1827 contained 11,000 vols. The annual average attendance of students at the two universities in Old and New Aberdeen is about 650. The total number of professors and lecturers is 28. The number of scholarships, bursarships, &c., 240. The average value of the college benefices £290. The total yearly revenue of the professors and lecturers £5,522; of the heads-of-houses or principals £600; of the college-scholarships £3,194. Total revenue of colleges and university £9,496. [H. L. Jones, in Statistical Journal, vol. i. p. 385.]—Gordon's hospital, founded in 1730, is a richly endowed establishment at which 112 boys are maintained and educated.—Three weekly newspapers are published in A.—The entire parish of St. Nicholas contains about 1,100 acres, of which considerably more than the half is occupied by the city of N. A.; the remainder is chiefly links and garden ground.

ABERDEENSHIRE, a county of Scotland, the fifth in respect of area, and the third in amount of pop. It is bounded on the NE and E by the German ocean; on the SE by Kincardineshire; on the S. by Forfarshire; on the SW by Perthshire; on the W and NW by Inverness-shire and Banffshire. Its greatest length is measured by a line extending WSW to ENE; or from Scarscoch, the SW point of Braemar, to Rattray-head, the eastern point of the bay of Fraserburgh, a distance of about 87 m. Its breadth from the mouth of the Dee on the E, to the headsprings of the Don on the W, is about 47 m., but in some places is much less. Its extent of sea-coast, l ABE from the mouth of the Dee, which separates it from Kincardineshire, to the stream which separates it from Banffshire, is nearly 70 m. Its superficial area has been roughly estimated at 1,970 sq. m. The SW parts of this county are extremely wild and mountainous, embracing the crest of the principal range of the Grampians, among which are Scarscoch, 3,402 ft; Cairn Taggart, 3,000 ft.; Loch-na-Gar, 3,815, or, according to some, 3,777 ft.; Mount Kean 3,126 ft.; Cairn Toul 4,245 ft.; Ben Macdhui 4,390 ft.; Benna-Buird 3,940 ft.; Ben Aven 2,934 ft. Towards the E and NE the face of the country is much less rugged.—The principal rivers, in the order of their outfall from S to N, and thence round to W, are the Dee, the Don, the Ythan, the Ugie, and the Deveron. All these rivers—which will be found described under their respective heads—flow into the German ocean. About 6,400 acres are occupied with small lochs. The only canal and railroad have been noticed in the preceding article. About one-fifth of the entire surface consists of high uncultivable mountain-tracts; and hills, moors, and other waste-lands occupy nearly two-thirds of the remainder. The general character of the county may consequently be described as bleak and uninviting; but there are many highly cultivated districts, particularly in the midland district, and along the courses of the rivers; and a great extent of waste land has been reclaimed of late years. Large forests of natural wood occur in some of the interior districts, especially in Braemar, Glentanner, and Mortlach. The Scotch fir and the larch are the prevailing forest trees. The flowering plants, including grasses, found in a range of 16 m. round Aberdeen, were reported in 1843 to amount to 287 genera, and 562 species. [Mag. of Nat. Hist., vol. xi, p. 73.]—The mean temperature of Aberdeen, from 19 years observation, is 47° 1'; at Buchanness, the easternmost point of the county and of the mainland of Scotland, in 1° 45' W long, 47° 3'; at Alford, 26 m. inland, and 420 ft. above sealevel, 40° 03'.—The pop. of the county in 1800 was 123,082; in 1811, 135,075; in 1821, 155,387; in 1831, 177,657; in 1841, 192,387 occupying 32,063 houses. Of the pop. of 1841, 166,352 were natives or the county, 1,711 were natives of England, and 1,037 of Ireland. The number of persons engaged in commerce, trade, and manufactures, in 1841, was 27,937 or 15.5 per cent.; in agriculture, 25,224 or 13.1 per cent. The flax and linen manufacture employed 3,489 hands; cotton manufactures 1,448; and that of worsted stockings 1,330. The increase per cent. of the pop. between 1831 and 1841 appears to have been only 8.2 per cent., whereas the average increase on the pop. of Scotland within that decade was 11.1 per cent.—The annual value of real property assessed in 1815 was £325,218; in 1842-3, £606,802. The county sends one member to parliament. Number of electors in 1842-3, 3,429.—Aberdeenshire contains the city and parliamentary burgh of Aberdeen; the royal burghs of Inverury, Kintore, and Peterhead; and the burghs-of-barony of Charleston, Fraserburgh, Huntly, Old Meldrum, Rosehearty, and Turriff.—The number of parishes is 90; besides 14 quoad sacra parishes.
ABERDEEN MOUNTAINS, a range of mountains on the east coast of Australia, running from SSW to NNE, parallel to the Wurragong or White Mountains, and further inland.

ABERDOUR, a p. in Aberdeenshire. Pop. in 1841, 1,645; whereof 376 were in the v. of New Aber dour, and 168 in the fishing village of Pennan.—Also a p. on the Forth coast of Fifeshire. Pop. in 1841, 1,916; whereof 307 were in the small fishing v. of East Aberdour, and 469 in that of West Aberdour, 21 m. W of Burntisland.—The island of Inchcolm belongs to this parish.
ABEREDW, a p. in Radnorshire. Pop. 345.
ABERERCH, a p. in Carnarvonshire. Pop. 1,613.
ABERFELDY, a v. in Perthshire, on the Tay, 64 m. NE of Kenmore. Pop. 823.
ABERFFRAW, a p. and sea-port in Anglesey. Pop. 1,336. It was anciently a place of considerable importance.
ABERFORD, a p. and v. in Yorkshire. Acres 3,820. Pop. 1,071; whereof 782 were in the v., which lies 3 m. N of the Leeds and Selby railroad.
ABERFORS, a picturesque v. on the route from Abo to St. Petersburgh, between Lovisa and Broby.
ABERFOYLE, a p. and hamlet in Perthshire, on the Teith, 18 m. W of Stirling. Pop. 543.

ABERGAVENNY, a p. and town in Monmouthshire. Area 4,290 acres. Pop. 4,953. The town, which in 1841 had a pop. of 2,720, is on the high road from Brecon to Monmouth, 20 m. from Merthyr, 22 from Newport, at the junction of the Gavenny with the Usk. Its principal trade is in wool. The Brecknock and A. canal branches off from the Monmouthshire canal a little to the S of Pontypool, and crosses the Avon, proceeds to Uske 141 m., and thence to Brecon, 18 m. The total length is 33 m., with a rise of 68 ft. About 31 m. NNE from the town is the Sugarloaf mountain which has an altitude above sea-level of 1,800 ft. The Blorenge, alt. 1,720 ft., and the Skynid-Mawr, alt. 1,498 ft., are also in the neighbourhood.
ABERGELE, a p. and sea-port in Denbighshire, 7 m. W of St. Asaph. Pop. 2,661; whereof 945 were in the township of Abergele.
ABERGEMENT LE PETIT, a v. of France, in the dep. of the Ain, 8 m. SSE of Mantua. ABERGEMENT (L'), SAINTE MARIE, a v. of France, in the dep. of Doubs, 8 m. SSW of Pontarlier. Pop. 490.

ABERGHU'N. See KURDISTA'N RIVER. ABERGU'H-the ABERKHU of German and ABERKOUH of French geographers-a fortified place, 34 m. E of Surmeh, in the Persian prov. of Fars, which has several villages under its control, and falls at present under the jurisdiction of Ispahan.—De Bode.
ABERGWILLY, a p. and v. in Carmarthenshire. Pop. 2,366. Acres 10,748. The v. is on the Gwilly, 2 m. E by N from Carmarthen.
ABERHAFESP, a p. in Montgomeryshire, 41 m N by W of Newton. Pop. 535.
ABERKENI, a town in the Turkish prov. of Roumelia, on a branch of the Tonja.

ABERLADY, a p. and v. in East Lothian. Pop. 1,050; of whom 537 were in the v. which is 51 m. NNW of Haddington near a shallow bay of the frith of Forth, into which a streamlet called the Peffer falls.
ABERLEMNO, a p. and v. in Forfarshire, on the South Esk. Pop. 1,009.

ABERLOUR, a p. and v. in Banffshire, on the south b. of the Spey. Pop. 1,352; whereof 328 were in the v. now called Charlestown, 12 m. above Craigellachie bridge. In the SW part of the p. rises Ben Rinnes, a mountain having an alt. of 2,765 ft. above sea-level, and 1,876 ft. above the subjacent country.
ABERLLUNVEY, a newly constituted p., heretofore a hamlet, in Glasbury p., in Breconshire. Pop. 116.
ABERNANT, a p. in Carmarthenshire. Pop. 890.

ABERNETHY, a p. and v. in Perthshire, at the junction of the Earn with the Tay. Pop. 1,920; of whom 827 were in the v which is represented by uniform tradition to be built on the site of the ancient capital of the Pictish kings. There is here one of those curious round towers of which so many occur in Ireland. It is 74 ft. high, and 16 in diameter. ABERNETHY a mountainous and extensive p. ABH partly in Elginshire, partly in Inverness-shire. Pop. 1,832. The mountain Cairn Gorm [alt. 4,081 ft. Playfair ] is in this parish. There are immense pine-forests in this district.
ABERNYTE, a p. in Perthshire. Pop. 290.
ABERPORTH, a p. and v. in Cardiganshire. Pop. 496. The v. is a small port on Cardigan bay, 7 m. NNE of Cardigan.
ABERRHIW. See BERRIEW.
ABERSEE. See ABENSEE.
ABERT (LAKE), a foetid salt lake, on the Cascade range of mountains, in the Oregon territory, in N lat. 42° 40'. It is about 20 m. in length from S to N.
ABERTANN, a mining v. of Bohemia in the circle and 12 m. NNE of Elnbogen, at the foot of the Hertz mountains. Pop. 900.
ABERWHEELER, a township and v. in the p. of Bodfary, Denbighshire. Pop. 534; of the v. 114.
ABERYSCIR, a p. in Breconshire, on the Yscir near its junction with the Usk. Pop. 117.

ABERYSTWITH, a p. and v. of Monmouthshire. Area 10,930 acres. Pop. in 1841, 11,272, being an increase of 5,280 on that of 1831, and chiefly attributable to the extension of mines in this district. The v. is 9 m. SW by W from Abergavenny.

ABERYSTWITH, a chapelry and burgh in Cardiganshire. Pop. 4,916. The burgh is pleasantly situated on Cardigan bay, near the junction of the Rheidol and Ystwith, 39 m. NE of Cardigan. The harbour has been recently improved. The shipping registered at the port in 1845 amounted to 7,824 tons; and the vessels which entered the port were 347 of a total tonnage of 10,936 tons. The income of the harbour in 1843 amounted to £1,571. The chief exports are lead-ore and pig-lead. In the autumn months the town is a fashionable watering-place. The parliamentary burgh of A. is contributory to the Cardigan district of burghs. Its pop. in 1841 was 4,975; number of electors in 1842-3, 218.
ABESGUN, or Abisagun, a small port on the S coast of the Caspian, in the Persian prov. of Mazanderán, 10 m. N of Asterabad.
ABESSINIA. See ABYSSINIA.
ABESTOS MOUNTAINS, a range in the Griqua territory, S Africa, to the N of the Orange river.

ABETSAI (CAPE), a promontory on the Circassian coast of the Black sea, 61 m. SSE of Ghelendjik. The coast is here very rugged, and the cliffs from this eastwards assume a reddish tint which they preserve uninterruptedly as far as Mingrelia.—Marigny.

ABEX, a name sometimes given by earlier Portuguese missionaries and geographers—and retained in such works as Rees's Cyclopedia and the Encyclopedia Britannica—to that portion of the western coast of the Red sea from Bab-el-Mandeb northwards to the frontier of Nubia, and of which Arkiko and Suakim are the chief ports. The word is the same as the Arabic Habesh, which is sometimes given to Abyssinia; and Telluz calls the Abyssinians Abexins; but the district in question appears to have long maintained a kind of political independence of Abyssinia Proper, and was governed by an Arab sheik, with the title of naib, under the Porte. It is at present in the possession of the Danákil and Adál tribes.
ABGARRIS ISLANDS, a group of several low isles to the eastward of New Ireland in Australasia. They extend from NE to SW 27 m., and were discovered in 1823 by a ship called the Abgarris.
ABHA, a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Tigre, 40 m. NE of Axum, on the route to Dixan, in N lat. 14° 44′, E long. 39° 20′.

ABHE'BBAD (LAKE), a lake of Eastern Africa, in the Shoa territory, in N lat. 11° 20', E long. 41° 45'. It is described as a day's foot-journey in length, and as containing seven islands. Its waters are said to emit a fœtid smell. The river Hawash is supposed to flow through it after passing Ausa and fetching a circuit round a high mountain called Damma 'Ali at its SW extremity. ABHER, HABAR, or EBHER, a city of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, on the great road from Zenjan to Kasbin, 20 m. ESE of Sultaniyeh. Rennell identifies this place with the Habar of 2 Kings xvii. 6; but Bell in a note to his edition of Rollin's Ancient History [vol. i. p. 135] contends against this opinion and in favour of Herab at the foot of the Ak-dagh. A. is remarkable as being the most eastern limit to which the Turkish is familiarly spoken. The Persian language commences here, and prevails eastwards to Hindostan.
ABI, a Kurdish word signifying river, both prefixed and affixed to numerous streams in Persia, Hindostan, and some adjacent districts, in the forms aba, abi, abo, obo, obu.
ABIA DE LAS TORRES, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Palencia, 10 m. SE of Saldana, on the r. bank of the Abanades, in lat. 42° 25' N.
ABIAD, a town of Dembea, on the route from Gondar to Sennár, 90 m. NNE of Gondar.

ABIAD (WAD-EL), a river of Algiers, which has its source 60 m. S of Kostantínah, in lat. 35° 22' N, on the SW side of Jebel Shelíyah, near the town of Medina, in Jebel Aures, and flows 70 m. SW and S to the Wad Jedi, which latter falls into the NW end of Sebkhat-el-Melghigh or the Salt marsh, in lat. 34° 26' N.—Carte d'Algerie du Bur. Top. Paris, 1847.

ABIAD (BAHR-EL), or WHITE RIVER, the direct, longest, and most important head-stream of the Nile; the Blue river or Astapus (Bruce's Nile), which joins it in 15° 37' N lat., being only a tributary. The recent Egyptian expeditions have ascended it as far as the kingdom of Bari, under the 4th parallel of N lat. Of these expeditions the most important is that in which D'Arnaud and Werne took part (1841). At the point where the expedition turned back, the river consisted of two arms, respectively 330 and 110 yards broad; while there were evident signs that, during the rains, the greater portion of the valley of the river, near 650 yards wide, is entirely covered with water. The natives of Bari informed Werne, and also Baron von Müller, who has since been there, that the river comes from a month's journey further S. Dr. Beke, from a comparison of various authorities, carries the main stream of the Nile up to the country of Mono-Moezi, in about 2° to 4° S lat., and from 29° to 34° E long., a district from which Ptolemy derived his Mountains of the Moon (Moezi); and he shows the great probability of its being connected with a lake in that country, which others had supposed to be identical with the great lake in the interior of Africa called N'yassi, i. e. the Sea, but which Dr. Krapf states to be a separate collection of water, and apparently the Lake Zambre of the Portuguese. On its r. bank, in 9° 20' N, the Bahr-el-Abiad receives a large river called Sobat, Telfi, or river of Habesh, which Dr. Beke identifies with the Uma or Godjeb, whose head-streams are in Enarea and Kaffa, one of them being the Zebee, crossed by Fernandez in 1613. In about the same latitude, but about 60 m. higher up its course, which is here from W to E, it forms a lake called Birket-el-Ghazal (No or Kura of the Arabs); here it receives a large arm from the W, named Bahr - el - Ghazal (Keilak), which appears to be the Nile of Herodotus and all other writers anterior to Ptolemy. The entire upper course of the river is a succession of extensive lakes and swamps, studded with numerous low islands, rather than a running stream, so that the Egyptian expeditions often found it difficult to trace its channel. The mass of waters, even in the dry season, proves the immense extent of the basin of the river, and of the vast number of head-streams from which it must almost incessantly be fed. Below the junction of the Sobat and Bahr-el-Ghazal, the inhabitants of the I. bank of the Bahr-el-Abiad are Shilluks, and of the r. bank Dinkas, which latter race continues along orgitized by Google ABI both sides of the river as far as about 6° N, where begin tribes connected by language with the natives of Bari. - Werne, Exped. zur Entdeckung der Quellen des Weissen Nil (Berlin, 1848); Beke, in Geog. Journ., vol. xviii.
ABIAD (JEBEL-EL), a mountain in the Syrian desert, about 22 m. NW of Palmyra.

ABIAD (RAS-EL), or CAPE BLANCO, a well known promontory on the Syrian coast, between Zib and Súr, the ancient Tyre, in N lat. 33° 5', E long. 35° 0'. -Also a prom. on the coast of Tunis, in E long. 9° 48', and N lat. 37° 20'. It forms the western horn of the gulf of Bizerta, and appears to have been the Promontorium candidum of the ancients. Shaw supposes it may have been the Promontorium pulchrum of Livy.
ABI-ALI'SHTAR, a riv. of Persia, flowing through a plain between Khorramábád and Kermánsháh.
AB'-ALLAH, a considerable stream in the Persian district of Susan, flowing into the Kurdistán river, in the plain of Ram Hormuz.

ABI-ATRAK, a river in the northern part of Persia, near the edge of the desert of Khiva, which rises near Kuchan, and flows 250 m. in a westerly direction, falling into the Caspian sea, 40 m. N of Astrabad.
ABI-BALA'D-RU'D, a river of Luristan, in Persia, which falls into the river of Dizful, 7 m. SW of that town, after a circuitous southern course of 40 m.

ABI-BORS, the principal tributary of the Karun river of Khúzistan. It is formed by the junction of the Kersán and 'Abi-Garin to the SW of Fellát, and is a broad and rapid stream. - Layard in Geog. Journ., xvi. 51.
ABICHU-GALLAS, a tribe of Gallas in possession of a district of Shoa to the W of Angolalla.
ABICHAI, a town of Chinese Tartary, on the 1. bank of the Upper Irtisch, in N lat. 47°.
ABICHAN, or ABYCHKAN, a lake of Western Siberia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, lying between Lake Tchany and Lake Soumy, with both of which it communicates, under the parallel of 54° N.
ABICHOR, a town of Little Tibet, situated in a defile to the E of Mount Pushti-Kur.
ABICUI, a town of Mexico, to the NE of Santa Fé.
ABID, or ABU'D, a small town of Arabia, in the Wady Rema, 12 m. S of Doran.
ABI'D (Sidi), a town of Algiers, on the r. bank of the Arkew, at its confluence with the Shellif.
ABI'D (WAD UL), a branch of the Ummur-rebi'eh river of Marocco, which it joins in lat. 32° 30' N.

ABIDA (JEBEL), a volcanic mountain of Abyssinia, in the Mudaito territory, in about 10° 9' N lat., and 41° E long. Kirk, who estimates its height at about 4,000 ft. above the plain, says, this mountain, with that of Aiyalo or Azalo, about 10 m. to the NW, "forms the centre of an extensive volcanic tract, from which sheets of lava have descended from all sides to the plain, forming a field of volcanic matter about 30 m. in diameter, studded with small cones, each showing a distinct crater."—Geog. Journ., vol. xii. p. 231.
